title: The Fathers love for His Son. Part 3
scripture_text: ‘’Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. But John tried to deter him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?’’ Jesus replied, “Let it be so now; it is proper for us to do this to fulfil all righteousness.” Then John consented. As soon as Jesus was baptized, he went up out of the water. At that moment heaven was opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting on him. And a voice from heaven said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.”
devotional:
1. The father expressed his love for Jesus in a very clear and bold way. In Mark’s account of the Baptism of Jesus we are told a very interesting thing. He says that the heavens were torn open. The word for torn is schizomenous, this is a violent word that means to tear or cleave and it requires determination and strength. The same word is used in Mark 15:38 talking about the curtain in the temple being torn in two. (Apparently the temple curtain was so heavy that it took 300 men to lift it.)

When the heavens tore open, it was as if God the Father was so determined to show his love to his Son; it was as if nothing would get in the way of God pouring his love upon his Jesus. The love the Father had for his son was always intentional.

Authentic love at its essence is always proactive, it will always choose to express itself. When Mark tells us the heavens were torn open, he was alluding to a passage in the Old Testament, Isaiah 64:1-4

‘Oh, that you would rend the heavens and come down, that the mountains would tremble before you! 2 As when fire sets twigs ablaze and causes water to boil,

Come down to make your name known to your enemies and cause the nations to quake before you! 3 or when you did awesome things that we did not expect,

you came down, and the mountains trembled before you. 4 Since ancient times no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you, who acts on behalf of those who wait for him.’

Isaiah 64:1-4 is part of a longer prayer of lament recorded in Isaiah 63:7-64:12. A lament is a prayer that cries out to God from the midst of desperate grief, pain, or any circumstance that seems out of control. It vocalizes the hurt to God with the conviction, and the faith, that God can, and will bring relief. A lament is not just the venting of frustration, but is a profound statement of faith in God from the midst of utter human hopelessness. The significance of a lament is that the worshipper prays in the midst of his pain. He believes that God cares about His condition and he has enough faith in God to trust Him with the outcome. When the heavens were torn open at the baptism of Jesus, it was God the father intervening into our history and pain.

2. The father expressed his love by affirming Jesus’ identity.

Knowing who we are and accepting who we are can be a difficult process. Often how we view ourselves is very different as to how God sees us. The word that is used for love is agapetos, which describes one who is in a very special relationship with another and in secular Greek is used mostly of a child, especially an only child to whom all the love of his parents is given (e.g. used by the Father describing His only Son and Abraham describing his "only son" in).Gen 22:2

In the world in which we live, identity is usually tied to a persons gender, abilities, achievements or successes. But biblically a persons identity has nothing to do with what we do, or what we achieve, but has everything to do with who we are. When Jesus was being baptized, he wasn't standing before his father with an impressive list of achievements that made him loveable. Jesus understood that he needed to receive his Father’s love if he was going to accomplish his father’s will.

For Jesus, accepting his identity was about him being able to be the person his father saw him to be. How God sees us is the most important thing about us. If you were to hear God speaking from heaven, and say ‘you are my son, my daughter,’ how would you react?

Most of the time when we find ourselves far from God, it can usually be traced back to some kind of identity crisis about how God sees us, or how we think God sees us. It is difficult to live as a child of God if you do not believe you are a child of God.

It is very interesting that immediately after the Father declared Jesus to be his Son, Jesus was taken into the wilderness where he was tempted by Satan. The whole point of the temptation was to question and undermine Jesus’ identity. Three times he begins each temptation with the same question, ‘If you are the Son of God?’ He was trying to convey the complete opposite to Jesus, as to what his father had done at his baptism. Temptation to sin is Satan’s attempt to rob us of our identity.
prayer: Father thank you that our identity is not about our achievements, but the fact that we are your children.
html:
The Fathers love for His Son. Part 3

Study Text: Matthew 3 13-17
‘’Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John. But John tried to deter him, saying, “I need to be baptized by you, and do you come to me?’’ Jesus replied, “Let it be so now; it is proper for us to do this to fulfil all righteousness.” Then John consented. As soon as Jesus was baptized, he went up out of the water. At that moment heaven was opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting on him. And a voice from heaven said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.”

1. The father expressed his love for Jesus in a very clear and bold way. In Mark’s account of the Baptism of Jesus we are told a very interesting thing. He says that the heavens were torn open. The word for torn is schizomenous, this is a violent word that means to tear or cleave and it requires determination and strength. The same word is used in Mark 15:38 talking about the curtain in the temple being torn in two. (Apparently the temple curtain was so heavy that it took 300 men to lift it.)

When the heavens tore open, it was as if God the Father was so determined to show his love to his Son; it was as if nothing would get in the way of God pouring his love upon his Jesus. The love the Father had for his son was always intentional.

Authentic love at its essence is always proactive, it will always choose to express itself. When Mark tells us the heavens were torn open, he was alluding to a passage in the Old Testament, Isaiah 64:1-4

‘Oh, that you would rend the heavens and come down, that the mountains would tremble before you! 2 As when fire sets twigs ablaze and causes water to boil,

Come down to make your name known to your enemies and cause the nations to quake before you! 3 or when you did awesome things that we did not expect,

you came down, and the mountains trembled before you. 4 Since ancient times no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you, who acts on behalf of those who wait for him.’

Isaiah 64:1-4 is part of a longer prayer of lament recorded in Isaiah 63:7-64:12. A lament is a prayer that cries out to God from the midst of desperate grief, pain, or any circumstance that seems out of control. It vocalizes the hurt to God with the conviction, and the faith, that God can, and will bring relief. A lament is not just the venting of frustration, but is a profound statement of faith in God from the midst of utter human hopelessness. The significance of a lament is that the worshipper prays in the midst of his pain. He believes that God cares about His condition and he has enough faith in God to trust Him with the outcome. In the world in which we live, identity is usually tied to a persons gender, abilities, achievements or successes. But biblically a persons identity has nothing to do with what we do, or what we achieve, but has everything to do with who we are. When Jesus was being baptized, he wasn't standing before his father with an impressive list of achievements that made him loveable. Jesus understood that he needed to receive his Father’s love if he was going to accomplish his father’s will.

For Jesus, accepting his identity was about him being able to be the person his father saw him to be. How God sees us is the most important thing about us. If you were to hear God speaking from heaven, and say ‘you are my son, my daughter,’ how would you react?

Most of the time when we find ourselves far from God, it can usually be traced back to some kind of identity crisis about how God sees us, or how we think God sees us. It is difficult to live as a child of God if you do not believe you are a child of God.

It is very interesting that immediately after the Father declared Jesus to be his Son, Jesus was taken into the wilderness where he was tempted by Satan. The whole point of the temptation was to question and undermine Jesus’ identity. Three times he begins each temptation with the same question, ‘If you are the Son of God?’ He was trying to convey the complete opposite to Jesus, as to what his father had done at his baptism. Temptation to sin is Satan’s attempt to rob us of our identity.

Prayer: Father thank you that our identity is not about our achievements, but the fact that we are your children.
Bible in a year reading plan: 2 Chronicles 16-18
